items.q: Roughly what proportion of energy passes from one trophic level to the next?
items.why: Students assume energy is conserved across levels. Most energy is lost as heat (respiration), through movement, indigestible parts, and uneaten material. The 10% rule explains why food chains are short.

items.q: Why is atmospheric nitrogen unavailable to most plants directly?
items.correct: N2 has a strong triple bond that most organisms cannot break.
items.misconception: Because plants cannot reach high enough to access it.
items.why: Students miss the chemistry. Despite 78% of air being N2, the molecule's triple bond is energetically costly to break. Only specialised nitrogen-fixing bacteria (and lightning, industrial Haber process) can convert it to usable forms.

items.q: Why does renewable energy require energy storage solutions?
items.correct: Solar and wind generation are intermittent - storage smooths out supply and demand.
items.misconception: Renewable energy can never meet demand.
items.random_wrong: Because they generate too much energy.
items.why: Students dismiss renewables as inadequate. The challenge is intermittency, not insufficiency - the Sun sets, wind drops. Storage (batteries, pumped hydro) addresses timing, not total capacity.

items.q: Hardin's 'Tragedy of the Commons' was published in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1968. “1962” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The Brundtland definition of sustainable development appeared in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1987. “1972” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Year of the Rio Earth Summit?
items.why: Exact answer: 1992 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
